stem: A healthy 9-year-old boy wakes up in the early morning and runs to his parents' room. He is conscious but unable to speak, and his parents note excessive drooling and rhythmic twitching of his right lower face. The episode lasts for 2 minutes and stops spontaneously. He has normal development and a normal neurologic exam. What is the most likely EEG finding for this patient?
explanation: This is a classic presentation of Benign Childhood Epilepsy with Centrotemporal Spikes (BECTS), also known as benign rolandic epilepsy. It is the most common idiopathic focal epilepsy in childhood, typically presenting between ages 5 and 10 with sleep-related simple partial seizures involving facial motor and sensory cortex (drooling, facial twitching, speech arrest). The characteristic EEG finding is bilateral, independent centrotemporal spike-and-wave activity.

stem: A 6-month-old girl presents with a 2-week history of spells where her head suddenly drops forward and her arms forcefully abduct and flex. These occur in clusters, primarily when she is waking up from sleep. Her parents note she has stopped smiling and no longer reaches for toys. An EEG reveals a chaotic, highly disorganized pattern of high-voltage slow waves and multifocal spikes. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
explanation: The infant has Infantile Spasms (West Syndrome), characterized by epileptic spasms, developmental regression, and an interictal EEG pattern known as hypsarrhythmia. ACTH and vigabatrin are the primary first-line treatments for infantile spasms.

stem: A 7-year-old girl is evaluated for a decline in her school performance. Her teacher reports that she frequently stares off into space and flutters her eyelids for 5 to 10 seconds before resuming her activities as if nothing happened. In the clinic, having the patient hyperventilate reproduces an episode. The first-line medication for her condition works primarily via which of the following mechanisms?
explanation: The patient has Childhood Absence Epilepsy, which features brief staring spells provoked by hyperventilation and a classic 3-Hz generalized spike-and-wave EEG pattern. Ethosuximide is a first-line treatment, and it exerts its antiepileptic effect by inhibiting T-type calcium channels in thalamocortical networks.

stem: Neurology / Neuro-oncology An 18-month-old, previously healthy boy develops a subacute onset of severe irritability, tremulousness, and chaotic, rapid conjugate eye movements. He is unable to walk due to severe ataxia. Which of the following diagnostic tests is most critical in identifying the underlying etiology of this syndrome?
explanation: The triad of opsoclonus (chaotic conjugate eye movements), myoclonus, and ataxia in a toddler is classic for Opsoclonus-Myoclonus Syndrome (OMS). In childhood, approximately 50% of OMS cases are a paraneoplastic phenomenon associated with an underlying neuroblastoma, making screening with urine catecholamines (HMA, VMA) and body imaging essential.

stem: A 6-month-old boy is evaluated for profound hypotonia. He was born at full term without complications but never achieved head control. On examination, he is alert and tracks faces well, but lies in a "frog-leg" posture. He has profound axial and appendicular hypotonia, absent deep tendon reflexes, and noticeable fasciculations of his tongue. Where does the primary pathology localize?
explanation: The presentation is highly suspicious for Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) Type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffmann disease). Alertness with normal extraocular movements makes a cerebral etiology less likely, while profound weakness, areflexia, and classic tongue fasciculations strongly localize the defect to the lower motor neurons in the anterior horn cells.

stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a 6-month history of behavioral outbursts, declining academic performance, and a new stiff, clumsy gait. His mother mentions his skin has become unusually tanned over the past year. Examination reveals cognitive impairment, hyperreflexia, bilateral Babinski signs, and optic disc pallor. An MRI of the brain shows symmetric hyperintense T2 signal changes in the parieto-occipital white matter. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: X-linked Adrenoleukodystrophy is a peroxisomal disorder caused by an *ABCD1* gene mutation, leading to the accumulation of very-long-chain fatty acids (VLCFAs). The classic presentation in school-aged boys includes cognitive and behavioral decline, progressive spastic paraparesis, visual loss, skin hyperpigmentation (adrenal insufficiency), and demyelination starting in the posterior (parieto-occipital) white matter.

stem: Vascular A 15-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department after the acute onset of left-sided facial droop and left arm and leg weakness. She had been playing on the monkey bars earlier that day. Examination shows left hemiparesis and a right-sided ptosis and miosis. An MRA confirms a right internal carotid artery dissection. The pupillary findings are the result of damage to which of the following?
choices: Parasympathetic fibers from the Edinger-Westphal nucleus
Sympathetic fibers traveling alongside the internal carotid artery
explanation: The patient has a right Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, and sometimes anhidrosis) secondary to a carotid artery dissection. Third-order sympathetic (postganglionic) fibers originate in the superior cervical ganglion and travel along the internal carotid artery through the cavernous sinus to innervate the pupillary dilators and Müller's muscle of the eyelid.

stem: A 16-year-old girl presents with left eye pain worsened by movement and decreased visual acuity. She mentions having an episode 6 months ago of numbness and weakness in her legs that spontaneously improved. Which of the following cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) findings would most strongly support a diagnosis of Multiple Sclerosis (MS) over Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis (ADEM)?
explanation: The patient has optic neuritis and a history of myelitis separated in time, highly suspicious for MS. While ADEM and MS can both present with demyelinating events, ADEM is typically a monophasic post-infectious illness, whereas MS is multiphasic. The presence of CSF oligoclonal bands (OCBs) is rare in ADEM but strongly supports the diagnosis of MS.

stem: A 17-year-old female with known relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis complains of double vision. On examination, when she attempts to look to the left, her right eye fails to adduct past the midline, and her left eye exhibits horizontal nystagmus. Convergence is preserved. Where is her new demyelinating lesion located?
explanation: The patient has a right Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia (INO). The MLF normally connects the contralateral abducens nucleus to the ipsilateral oculomotor nucleus to coordinate conjugate horizontal gaze. An INO is characterized by impaired adduction of the eye ipsilateral to the MLF lesion (right eye, right MLF), with abducting nystagmus in the contralateral eye.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ICU in nonconvulsive status epilepticus. The physician wishes to load an intravenous antiepileptic drug to gain control of the seizures. The child has a known history of severe developmental delay, stroke-like episodes, and lactic acidosis, raising high clinical suspicion for a mitochondrial disorder (MELAS). Which of the following antiepileptic drugs carries a high risk of fatal hepatotoxicity in this patient and must be strictly avoided?
explanation: Valproic acid carries a severe risk of fatal hepatotoxicity, particularly in children under 2 years of age on polytherapy and in children with underlying mitochondrial disorders (such as Alpers syndrome or MELAS) or fatty acid oxidation defects. It should be strictly avoided in this patient. Here is the next batch of 15 high-yield multiple-choice questions, building upon the previous set. This batch continues to focus heavily on child neurology while integrating epilepsy, pharmacology, neuroanatomy, and pediatric-to-adult crossover topics. Child Neurology Board Review Bank: Batch 2 (Questions 11–25)

stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a history of multiple seizure types, including sudden drop attacks and atypical absence seizures. His parents report that he has also experienced significant developmental delay over the past year. An EEG demonstrates a slow spike-and-wave pattern. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: Benign childhood epilepsy with centrotemporal spikes
explanation: Lennox-Gastaut syndrome is characterized by the classic triad of multiple seizure types (notably tonic seizures and drop attacks), slow spike-and-wave discharges on EEG, and intellectual disability. The peak age of onset is between 3 to 5 years.

stem: A 6-year-old girl is evaluated for language regression. Previously developing normally, she no longer responds to her name and has stopped speaking over the past few months. An audiogram is normal. An overnight EEG shows frequent epileptiform discharges during sleep. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Landau-Kleffner syndrome, also known as acquired epileptic aphasia, typically presents in children aged 5 to 7 years with both receptive and expressive language failure (verbal auditory agnosia) following a period of normal development. It is heavily associated with frequent epileptiform discharges during sleep, and the risk of residual language impairment is high.

stem: A 3-day-old neonate develops severe lethargy, hypotonia, myoclonic seizures, and frequent, intractable hiccups. The EEG shows a burst-suppression pattern. Laboratory testing reveals elevated glycine levels in both the serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). A CSF-to-serum glycine ratio of >0.6 is diagnostic for which condition?
explanation: Glycine encephalopathy (formerly nonketotic hyperglycinemia) presents in the newborn period with rapid progression to encephalopathy, hypotonia, myoclonic seizures, and characteristic intractable hiccups. The diagnosis is confirmed by an elevated CSF-to-serum glycine ratio, which is typically >0.6 in this condition (normal is <0.04).

stem: An 8-year-old boy with focal epilepsy is started on carbamazepine. After achieving excellent seizure control for the first three weeks, he begins having seizures again. His parents deny missing any doses. His carbamazepine serum level is drawn and found to be subtherapeutic. What pharmacokinetic property best explains this finding?
explanation: Carbamazepine is a hepatic enzyme inducer that undergoes autoinduction, meaning it induces the hepatic enzymes responsible for its own metabolism. This process takes 3 to 5 weeks at a single dose, which often leads to a drop in serum levels and necessitates a dose increase after the first month of therapy.

stem: Neurology (Congenital Malformations) A newborn is noted to have a lumbosacral myelomeningocele at birth. A subsequent MRI of the brain reveals downward displacement of the cerebellar vermis and tonsils, along with brainstem tissue, into the foramen magnum. Which of the following is the most accurate diagnosis for the brain malformation?
explanation: Chiari II malformation (Arnold-Chiari malformation) involves the downward displacement of the cerebellar vermis, tonsils, and brainstem tissue through the foramen magnum. It is almost always accompanied by a myelomeningocele. Symptoms often include prominent brainstem dysfunction and hydrocephalus.

stem: A 16-year-old boy presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss and tinnitus. MRI of the brain reveals bilateral enhancing masses in the cerebellopontine angles. The gene mutated in this condition normally encodes for which of the following proteins?
explanation: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas (located in the cerebellopontine angle) are the hallmark of Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2). NF2 is an autosomal dominant condition caused by a mutation on chromosome 22 of the NF2 gene, which encodes the cytoskeletal tumor suppressor protein merlin.

stem: A 3-month-old girl with a history of infantile spasms is evaluated in the clinic. A Wood's lamp examination reveals several hypopigmented macules. An echocardiogram shows multiple cardiac rhabdomyomas. This patient's genetic mutation most likely results in the inability to inhibit which cellular pathway?
explanation: Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC) presents with infantile spasms, hypopigmented macules (ash-leaf spots), and cardiac rhabdomyomas. It is caused by mutations in either the TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin) genes. Normally, the hamartin-tuberin complex functions to inhibit the mTOR pathway, meaning a mutation leads to uncontrolled cell growth.

stem: A 9-month-old infant of Ashkenazi Jewish descent presents with motor regression, an exaggerated startle response, and a cherry-red spot on the macula. Abdominal examination reveals marked hepatosplenomegaly. Which of the following enzyme deficiencies is the most likely cause?
explanation: Both Tay-Sachs disease (Hexosaminidase A deficiency) and Sandhoff disease (Hexosaminidase A and B deficiency) present with motor regression, exaggerated startle, and macular cherry-red spots. However, Sandhoff disease uniquely affects visceral organs in addition to the CNS, leading to hepatosplenomegaly.

stem: Cranial Nerves A 12-year-old girl develops a cavernous sinus thrombosis resulting in right eye ophthalmoplegia and ptosis. Which of the following sensory distributions is most likely to be affected by an accompanying cranial neuropathy in this anatomical region?
choices: Forehead and upper half of the cornea
explanation: The cavernous sinus contains cranial nerves III, IV, VI, the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (V1), and sympathetic fibers. V1 provides sensation to the forehead, vertex of the scalp, and the upper half of the cornea. The maxillary division (V2) innervates the cheek and upper lip, and exits the cranium via the foramen rotundum.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is evaluated for difficulty climbing stairs and frequent falls. On examination, he uses his hands to walk up his legs when rising from the floor (Gowers' sign). His calves appear enlarged. Serum creatine kinase (CK) is 50 times the upper limit of normal. What is the inheritance pattern of this condition?
explanation: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy is the most common genetic muscle disease and is inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern. It is caused by a mutation in the dystrophin gene, typically presenting in early childhood with proximal weakness, calf pseudohypertrophy, and massively elevated CK levels.

stem: A 14-year-old girl develops acute bilateral vision loss followed a week later by severe paraparesis, sensory loss below the umbilicus, and urinary retention. MRI of the spine shows a continuous hyperintense T2 signal extending from T3 to T8. The pathogenesis of this disease is most closely associated with an immune target located in which regions of the brain?
choices: Myelin basic protein in the deep white matter
Aquaporin-4 water channels in the brainstem and hypothalamus
explanation: Neuromyelitis optica (NMO or Devic's disease) is an inflammatory demyelinating disease affecting the optic nerves and spinal cord, often causing longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM extending >3 spinal segments). It is associated with antibodies against aquaporin-4 (AQP4), and brain lesions tend to cluster in areas with a high concentration of AQP4, such as the brainstem and hypothalamus.

stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with morning headaches, vomiting, and a wide-based, unsteady gait. MRI reveals a solid, enhancing mass in the cerebellar vermis. Histopathology shows a hypercellular sheet of small blue cells with pseudo-Homer-Wright rosettes. Given the tumor's typical biological behavior, what additional imaging is absolutely essential?
choices: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
PET scan of the whole body
explanation: Medulloblastoma is an embryonal tumor (small blue cell tumor) of the cerebellum that accounts for 20% of pediatric brain tumors. Because it has a high propensity to metastasize and spread via the CSF pathways (drop metastases), an MRI of the entire neuraxis (brain and spine) is critical for staging.

stem: A 9-month-old infant with a red, flat vascular birthmark covering the right forehead and upper eyelid presents with new-onset focal seizures of the left arm. Brain MRI shows right parietal and occipital leptomeningeal enhancement. What classical radiographic finding may develop on a head CT as this child ages?
explanation: Sturge-Weber syndrome is characterized by a facial port-wine stain (typically involving the V1 trigeminal distribution and inner canthus of the eye) and ipsilateral leptomeningeal angiomatosis, which often leads to epilepsy and hemiparesis. Over time, the affected brain parenchyma develops characteristic "tram-track" or gyral calcifications.

stem: A 3-year-old girl with previously normal development presents with progressive clumsiness, frequent falls, and a loss of language skills. Examination shows spasticity in her legs but absent deep tendon reflexes at the knees and ankles. MRI of the brain shows symmetric T2 hyperintensities in the periventricular white matter that spare the U-fibers. Laboratory testing will most likely show a deficiency of which enzyme?
explanation: Metachromatic leukodystrophy is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a deficiency of arylsulfatase A, leading to sulfatide accumulation. It causes widespread demyelination of both the central nervous system (brain white matter changes sparing the U-fibers) and the peripheral nervous system, which explains the unique clinical presentation of upper motor neuron signs (spasticity) mixed with lower motor neuron signs (absent deep tendon reflexes).

stem: Movement Disorders A 16-year-old boy complains that his hands shake noticeably when he tries to drink from a glass or hold a cafeteria tray, causing him embarrassment at school. The shaking resolves when his hands rest in his lap. His father has a similar tremor. The patient notes that his father's tremor improves temporarily after drinking a glass of wine. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Essential tremor typically presents as a bilateral postural and kinetic tremor (4-8 Hz). It is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern with high penetrance, is often exacerbated by anxiety, and characteristically improves temporarily with alcohol consumption. Here is the next batch of 15 high-yield multiple-choice questions for your child neurology board review bank. This set continues to balance epilepsy, general child neurology, neuroanatomy, pharmacology, and adult/pediatric crossover topics. Child Neurology Board Review Bank: Batch 3 (Questions 26–40)

stem: A 5-year-old boy wakes up in the middle of the night looking pale and terrified. He complains of nausea, vomits once, and then exhibits unresponsiveness with rightward eye deviation that lasts for 5 minutes. His EEG during sleep shows high-voltage spike-and-wave discharges localized to the posterior regions of the brain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: Benign childhood epilepsy with centrotemporal spikes (BECTS)
explanation: Panayiotopoulos syndrome is a common benign idiopathic focal epilepsy of early childhood (peak age 3-6 years). It classically presents with autonomic symptoms (pallor, nausea, prominent vomiting, flushing, tachycardia) followed by eye deviation and impairment of consciousness. The characteristic EEG finding consists of occipital spikes, which are heavily activated by sleep.

stem: A 1-month-old male infant presents with frequent, intractable tonic spasms occurring dozens of times per day. His MRI shows evidence of a severe cortical malformation (hemimegalencephaly). An EEG demonstrates a burst-suppression pattern that is present continuously during both wakefulness and sleep. What is the most accurate diagnosis?
explanation: Ohtahara syndrome typically presents in the first 3 months of life (often within the first few days) with frequent tonic spasms and a continuous burst-suppression pattern on EEG during both wakefulness and sleep. It is most commonly associated with structural brain malformations. In contrast, Early Myoclonic Encephalopathy (EME) features myoclonic seizures, is more often linked to inborn errors of metabolism, and its burst-suppression pattern is often limited to sleep.

stem: Infectious Disease A 10-year-old immigrant boy with a history of delayed immunizations presents with declining school performance, behavioral changes, and new-onset generalized myoclonic jerks. His EEG reveals bilaterally symmetric, synchronous, high-voltage periodic complexes of sharp and slow waves repeating every 4 to 10 seconds. These bursts correlate with his myoclonic jerks. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: SSPE is a progressive, fatal neurodegenerative disease caused by a persistent, defective measles virus infection. It classically occurs years after the primary infection. The EEG finding of periodic high-voltage sharp and slow-wave complexes occurring every 4 to 10 seconds, time-locked with myoclonic jerks, is virtually diagnostic in the appropriate clinical setting.

stem: A 9-year-old girl with childhood absence epilepsy and generalized tonic-clonic seizures is currently treated with valproic acid. Her seizures are relatively well-controlled, but due to weight gain, her neurologist decides to slowly add lamotrigine as adjunctive therapy. Which of the following pharmacokinetic interactions must be carefully managed to reduce the risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome?
choices: Valproic acid induces hepatic cytochrome P450, decreasing lamotrigine levels.
Valproic acid inhibits the hepatic glucuronidation of lamotrigine, significantly increasing lamotrigine levels.
Lamotrigine displaces valproic acid from plasma proteins, causing fatal hepatotoxicity.
Lamotrigine induces the metabolism of valproic acid, leading to breakthrough seizures.
explanation: Valproic acid inhibits the hepatic glucuronidation of lamotrigine, prolonging its half-life and sharply increasing its serum concentration. When these drugs are used concomitantly, the starting dose of lamotrigine must be drastically reduced, and the titration schedule must be remarkably slow to minimize the risk of a severe allergic rash, such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome.

stem: A 16-year-old girl with a history of severe menstrual migraines is prescribed a triptan for acute abortive therapy. The primary mechanism of action of this class of medications involves which of the following?
choices: Non-selective antagonism of dopamine D2 receptors
Selective agonism of serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors
Reuptake inhibition of serotonin and norepinephrine
explanation: Triptans (e.g., sumatriptan) are selective agonists of 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors. They work by causing intracranial vasoconstriction (5-HT1B), inhibiting peripheral pain signal transmission and neuropeptide release (5-HT1D), and inhibiting central pain transmission via presynaptic dorsal horn stimulation. Due to their vasoconstrictive properties, they are contraindicated in patients with coronary artery disease or prior stroke.

stem: A 4.2 kg (9 lb 4 oz) neonate is born via vaginal delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia. In the nursery, the infant's right arm is held internally rotated and adducted at the shoulder, with the elbow extended and the wrist flexed (the "waiter's tip" posture). Grasp reflex is intact. This injury predominantly involves which of the following neural structures?
choices: Posterior cord of the brachial plexus
explanation: This describes Erb's palsy, an upper brachial plexus injury involving the C5 and C6 nerve roots. It is typically caused by lateral traction on the neck during delivery. The resulting weakness of the deltoid, infraspinatus, biceps, and wrist extensors leads to the classic "waiter's tip" posture. A C8-T1 injury (Klumpke palsy) would present with hand intrinsic weakness and an absent grasp reflex, often with an ipsilateral Horner syndrome.

stem: A 14-year-old boy undergoes a left anterior temporal lobectomy for medically refractory focal epilepsy. Post-operatively, formal visual field testing is most likely to reveal which of the following deficits?
explanation: The inferior fibers of the optic radiation (Meyer's loop) sweep forward into the temporal lobe before coursing back to the occipital cortex. These fibers carry visual information from the contralateral superior visual field. Therefore, a lesion or surgical resection in the left temporal lobe will disrupt the left Meyer's loop, producing a right superior homonymous quadrantanopia ("pie in the sky" deficit).

stem: A 15-year-old boy develops painless weakness in his right hand. On examination, he has weakness of finger and thumb extension. When asked to extend his wrist, the hand deviates strongly in a radial direction. Sensory examination of the hand and forearm is entirely normal. Where does the lesion localize?
choices: Radial nerve at the spiral groove
explanation: The Posterior Interosseous Nerve (PIN) is a pure motor branch of the radial nerve. It innervates the extensor digitorum communis, extensor carpi ulnaris (ECU), and thumb extensors. Because the branch to the extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis comes off the radial nerve proximal to the PIN, wrist extension is preserved but deviates radially due to the paralyzed ECU. Furthermore, because the superficial radial nerve branches off prior to the PIN, a PIN lesion causes no sensory deficits.

stem: An 18-year-old woman presents with acute left eye pain worsened by eye movement, followed by central vision loss. She is diagnosed with optic neuritis. An MRI of the brain shows multiple T2-hyperintense periventricular white matter lesions. According to the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial (ONTT), intravenous methylprednisolone therapy will have which of the following effects?
choices: It improves the final visual outcome at 5 years.
It speeds up the rate of visual recovery but does not alter the long-term visual outcome.
It increases the risk of recurrent optic neuritis compared to oral prednisone.
It permanently prevents the development of Multiple Sclerosis (MS).
explanation: The ONTT demonstrated that high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone followed by an oral taper speeds the rate of visual recovery and delays the onset of clinically definite MS for the first 2 years. However, by 5 to 10 years, there is no difference in the final visual outcome or the ultimate rate of conversion to MS compared to placebo. Notably, the trial also found that treating with oral prednisone alone increased the risk of recurrent optic neuritis.

stem: A 2-month-old male infant presents with profound hypotonia, poor feeding, a large anterior fontanelle, and a prominent forehead. Examination reveals marked hepatomegaly. Radiographs of the lower extremities demonstrate calcific stippling of the epiphyses. Serum very-long-chain fatty acids (VLCFAs) are severely elevated. This patient's condition is caused by a defect in which of the following cellular organelles?
explanation: This infant has Zellweger syndrome (cerebrohepatorenal syndrome), a severe disorder of peroxisomal biogenesis. It is characterized by craniofacial dysmorphism (high forehead, large fontanelle), profound hypotonia, hepatomegaly, seizures, and characteristic calcific stippling of the epiphyses. The absence of functional peroxisomes leads to the accumulation of VLCFAs.

stem: A 1-week-old female infant develops unprovoked focal seizures. Physical examination reveals an erythematous, vesicular rash distributed in linear and whorled patterns along the limbs and trunk following Blaschko lines. Her mother mentions having a history of multiple spontaneous abortions of male fetuses. What is the inheritance pattern of this infant's suspected disorder?
explanation: Incontinentia Pigmenti (Bloch-Sulzberger syndrome) is an X-linked dominant neurocutaneous disorder caused by a mutation in the NEMO (IKBKG) gene. It is typically lethal in males, explaining the mother's history of male miscarriages. In affected females, it causes seizures, developmental delay, and a characteristic skin rash that evolves through vesicular, verrucous, and hyperpigmented stages along Blaschko lines.

stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with morning headaches, vomiting, and a progressively unsteady gait. An MRI of the brain demonstrates a well-circumscribed, partially cystic mass with an intensely enhancing mural nodule in the cerebellar hemisphere. Histopathology shows a biphasic fibrillary background with eosinophilic granular bodies and Rosenthal fibers. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Pilocytic astrocytoma (WHO Grade I) is the most common pediatric central nervous system tumor and frequently arises in the cerebellum. Radiographically, it classically appears as a cyst with an enhancing mural nodule. The histological hallmarks are Rosenthal fibers (thick, eosinophilic, worm-like structures) and eosinophilic granular bodies in a fibrillary background.

stem: Neurology (Metabolic / Movement Disorders) A 1-year-old boy with a history of mild motor delay presents with acute developmental regression, generalized weakness, and a left abducens nerve palsy following a diarrheal illness. Brain MRI shows symmetric T2 hyperintensities in the putamen and periaqueductal gray matter. Serum and CSF lactate levels are markedly elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Leigh disease is a devastating mitochondrial encephalomyopathy that often presents in infancy with developmental regression, brainstem dysfunction (cranial neuropathies, respiratory issues), and hypotonia, typically triggered by a physiological stressor like a viral illness. The MRI hallmarks are bilateral, symmetric hyperintensities in the basal ganglia (putamen) and brainstem (periaqueductal gray), accompanied by elevated lactate in the blood and CSF.

stem: Neurology (Neuromuscular / Channelopathy) A 15-year-old girl complains of muscle stiffness and difficulty releasing her grip on doorknobs. She notes that the stiffness paradoxically improves after she repeatedly opens and closes her hands ("warm-up" phenomenon). Examination shows percussion myotonia of the thenar eminence. An EMG demonstrates diffuse myotonic discharges. This condition is most commonly caused by a mutation in a gene encoding for which of the following?
explanation: The patient has Myotonia Congenita (Thomsen or Becker disease), a channelopathy caused by mutations in the voltage-gated chloride channel gene (CLCN1). It is characterized by clinical and electrical myotonia that uniquely improves with repeated muscle action (the "warm-up" phenomenon). In contrast, Paramyotonia Congenita (a sodium channelopathy, SCN4A) worsens with exercise and cold exposure.

stem: Neurology / Psychiatry (ADHD & Tics) A 9-year-old boy with a known history of Tourette syndrome is brought to the clinic for worsening academic performance. His teachers report severe inattention, distractibility, and inability to stay in his seat. His parents are hesitant to start stimulant medications because they have read it might exacerbate his motor tics. Which of the following medications is a norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor that is FDA-approved for this comorbid condition and carries a lower risk of exacerbating tics?
explanation: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor approved for the treatment of ADHD. Because it does not directly increase dopamine in the striatum like stimulants (methylphenidate, dextroamphetamine) do, it is less likely to exacerbate tics, making it an excellent pharmacological choice for children with comorbid ADHD and Tourette syndrome. Here is the next batch of 15 high-yield multiple-choice questions for your child neurology board review bank. This set continues our focus on pediatric topics while mixing in high-yield epilepsy, neuroanatomy, pharmacology, and adult/pediatric crossover material. Child Neurology Board Review Bank: Batch 4 (Questions 41–55)

stem: Neurology (Embryology / Malformations) A newborn is found to have a single midline cerebral ventricle, fused thalami, and cyclopia. Failure of which embryonic vesicle to divide is responsible for this malformation?
explanation: Holoprosencephaly results from the failure of the prosencephalon to divide into the telencephalon and diencephalon, and the subsequent failure of the diencephalon to divide into two distinct hemispheres. It is strongly associated with midline facial defects (such as cyclopia and proboscis) and is frequently caused by chromosomal defects (Trisomy 13) or mutations in the SHH (Sonic Hedgehog) gene.

stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with recurrent, prolonged febrile hemiclonic seizures. By age 2, the child develops multiple seizure types including myoclonic and atypical absence seizures, along with noticeable developmental delay. Genetic testing is most likely to reveal a mutation in which of the following genes?
explanation: Severe Myoclonic Epilepsy of Infancy (Dravet syndrome) typically presents in the first year of life with prolonged, often febrile, focal or hemiclonic seizures. It later progresses to include multiple seizure types (myoclonic, atypical absence, etc.) and cognitive decline. The most frequent genetic cause is an autosomal dominant mutation in the SCN1A gene.

stem: A 7-year-old girl is evaluated by a neurologist. She is noted to have 8 café-au-lait macules, distinct axillary freckling, and multiple iris hamartomas (Lisch nodules). Which of the following central nervous system tumors is she at the highest risk of developing?
explanation: This patient meets the diagnostic criteria for Neurofibromatosis Type 1 (NF1). Patients with NF1 are at a significantly increased risk of developing optic pathway gliomas, which are a subset of pilocytic astrocytomas (WHO grade I).

stem: A 5-day-old neonate presents with poor feeding, lethargy, alternating hypotonia and hypertonia, and seizures. The urine has a distinct sweet, burnt-sugar odor. Elevated levels of branched-chain amino acids are found in the blood. Some intermittent cases of this disorder may respond to supplementation with which of the following?
explanation: Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD) presents in the neonatal period with encephalopathy and a classic urine odor. It is caused by a defect in the branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex, leading to elevated branched-chain amino acids in the blood and urine. Some intermittent cases are responsive to thiamine (Vitamin B1) supplementation.

stem: A 12-year-old boy from an immigrant family with an unknown vaccination history presents with declining academic performance, behavioral changes, and prominent myoclonic jerks. EEG reveals periodic high-amplitude slow-wave complexes occurring every 4 to 10 seconds. He had a severe febrile rash illness at age 1. What is the characteristic pathological finding in this patient's brain?
choices: Nuclear and cytoplasmic viral inclusion bodies in neurons
explanation: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) is a fatal demyelinating disease caused by a defective measles virus infection that occurred years earlier (highest risk if initially infected <2 years of age). The EEG classically shows periodic slow-wave complexes. Pathology demonstrates neurons containing nuclear and cytoplasmic viral inclusion bodies.

stem: A 16-year-old boy suddenly becomes deeply comatose. MRI of the brain reveals acute bilateral, symmetric infarctions in the paramedian thalami. This pattern of infarction is most likely due to occlusion of a single anatomic variant artery arising from which of the following parent vessels?
choices: P1 segment of the posterior cerebral artery (PCA)
P2 segment of the posterior cerebral artery (PCA)
explanation: Bilateral medial thalamic infarcts are classically caused by the occlusion of the Artery of Percheron. This is a normal anatomic variant where a single vessel arises from the P1 segment of one posterior cerebral artery (PCA) to supply the medial thalami bilaterally.

stem: A 9-year-old boy is started on carbamazepine for focal epilepsy. Three weeks later, his seizures return despite strict adherence to the prescribed dosage. His serum carbamazepine level is found to be subtherapeutic. What pharmacokinetic property of carbamazepine explains this phenomenon?
explanation: Carbamazepine is a hepatic enzyme inducer that undergoes autoinduction, meaning it induces the hepatic enzymes responsible for its own metabolism. This process typically takes 3 to 5 weeks at a single dose, which often results in a drop in serum levels and requires a dose increase after the first month of therapy.

stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with delayed walking, frequent falls, and calf pseudohypertrophy. When getting up from the floor, he uses his hands to push himself up his legs. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the dystrophin gene. What is the inheritance pattern of this disorder?
explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is the most common genetic muscle disease and is inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern. It predominantly affects proximal muscles and causes calf pseudohypertrophy due to fibrosis. It is caused by a mutation (1/3 are de novo) in the dystrophin gene.

stem: A 5-year-old boy with previously normal development presents with progressive loss of receptive and expressive language skills. He appears to no longer understand when spoken to, though his audiogram is completely normal. He has only had one brief clinical seizure. This clinical presentation, characteristic of Landau-Kleffner syndrome, is most accurately described as which of the following?
explanation: Landau-Kleffner syndrome is an epileptic encephalopathy characterized by acquired epileptic aphasia. Children present with acquired verbal auditory agnosia (combined receptive and expressive language failure) following a period of normal development. Up to 30% of patients do not have any clinical seizures, but they carry a very high risk of residual language impairment.

stem: Neurology (Metabolic/Leukodystrophy) A 5-month-old infant presents with extreme irritability, hypersensitivity to stimuli, hypertonicity evolving into opisthotonos, and unexplained low-grade fevers. MRI reveals symmetric periventricular white matter signal abnormality. CSF protein is elevated. Brain pathology demonstrates PAS-positive multinucleated macrophages. What is the underlying enzyme deficiency?
explanation: Krabbe disease (globoid cell leukodystrophy) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a mutation in the galactocerebrosidase gene. The infantile form presents at 4-6 months with irritability, hypertonicity/opisthotonos, unexplained fevers, and blindness. Pathology classically shows clusters of globoid cells, which are PAS-positive multinucleated macrophages with cytoplasmic accumulation of galactocerebroside.

stem: A 15-year-old girl develops a foot drop after wearing a tight cast for a fibular fracture. On examination, she has weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and foot eversion. Which of the following findings on physical examination would specifically localize the lesion to the common peroneal nerve rather than an L5 radiculopathy?
choices: Sensory loss over the lateral calf
Loss of the ankle jerk reflex
explanation: A common peroneal neuropathy causes weakness of ankle dorsiflexion (deep peroneal branch) and foot eversion (superficial peroneal branch). Foot inversion is mediated by the tibialis posterior muscle, which is innervated by the tibial nerve (medial division of the sciatic nerve) via L4 and L5 roots. Therefore, the preservation of foot inversion distinguishes a peripheral peroneal neuropathy from an L5 radiculopathy (where foot inversion would be impaired).

stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with progressive in-turning of her feet and difficulty walking. Her parents note that she walks relatively normally in the morning but develops severe gait difficulties and dystonic posturing of her legs by the late afternoon and evening. She exhibits mild parkinsonian features on examination. Which of the following is the most likely genetic etiology of her condition?
choices: Autosomal dominant mutation in the Torsin A (DYT1) gene
X-linked mutation in the DYT3 gene
Autosomal dominant mutation in the GTP cyclohydrolase 1 (GCH1) gene
Trinucleotide repeat expansion in the ATXN3 gene
explanation: Dopa-responsive dystonia (Segawa syndrome) classically presents in childhood (more commonly in females) with diurnal variation of dystonia (worse in the afternoon/evening) and mild parkinsonism. It is an autosomal dominant condition most commonly caused by a mutation in the GTP cyclohydrolase 1 (GCH1) gene on chromosome 14, leading to a defect in tetrahydrobiopterin synthesis (a cofactor for dopamine synthesis). It responds exquisitely to low-dose levodopa.

stem: A 14-year-old African American girl presents with acute, severe bilateral vision loss followed a month later by paraplegia and loss of bladder control. MRI of the spine reveals a contiguous T2-hyperintense lesion extending from the C5 to T2 spinal cord segments. Serum testing for aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies is positive. If brain lesions are present in this condition, they tend to preferentially involve which areas?
explanation: Neuromyelitis optica (Devic's disease) is characterized by severe optic neuritis (often bilateral) and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM, extending >3 segments). It is driven by antibodies against the aquaporin-4 water channel. When brain lesions occur in NMO, they typically localize to areas rich in AQP4 concentration, such as the hypothalamus and brainstem.

stem: Neurology (Metabolic/Leukodystrophy) A 10-month-old boy is evaluated for macrocephaly, developmental delay, and onset of seizures. MRI of the brain demonstrates extensive white matter T2-hyperintensity with a prominent anterior-to-posterior gradient (frontal lobe predominance). Pathology would be expected to show accumulation of Rosenthal fibers. This condition is caused by a mutation in the gene encoding which of the following proteins?
explanation: Alexander disease is a leukodystrophy characterized by infantile onset of macrocephaly, seizures, and psychomotor slowing. MRI classically shows extensive white matter changes with a frontal predominance. It is caused by a mutation in the GFAP (glial fibrillary acidic protein) gene, leading to a progressive disorder of astrocytes and the characteristic accumulation of Rosenthal fibers.

stem: A 2-week-old male neonate presents with frequent, intractable tonic spasms occurring dozens of times per day. The EEG demonstrates a burst-suppression pattern that is present during both wakefulness and sleep. Neuroimaging reveals a severe structural brain malformation (hemimegalencephaly). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Ohtahara syndrome (early infantile epileptic encephalopathy) presents between day 1 and month 3 of life with frequent tonic spasms and a continuous burst-suppression pattern on EEG during both wakefulness and sleep. It is heavily associated with structural brain malformations. In contrast, Early Myoclonic Encephalopathy (EME) features predominant myoclonic seizures, is more often metabolic in origin, and burst-suppression is most prominent during sleep. Here is the fifth batch of 15 high-yield multiple-choice questions for your child neurology board review bank. This batch continues to blend general child neurology, genetic/metabolic conditions, epilepsy, neuroanatomy, pharmacology, and relevant crossover topics. Child Neurology Board Review Bank: Batch 5 (Questions 56–70)

stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with brief, sudden jerks of his arms and shoulders. His parents note these spells are most frequently provoked by sudden noises or when he is drowsy just before falling asleep. The jerks happen in isolation, never in clusters. His development and neurologic exam are entirely normal. An EEG demonstrates generalized spike-and-wave discharges. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Benign myoclonic epilepsy in infancy is characterized by the onset of brief myoclonic seizures between ages 4 months and 3 years, occurring in normally developing children. These seizures are easily provoked by drowsiness, photic stimulation, or external startle, and importantly, they do not occur in clusters (which helps clinically distinguish them from the epileptic spasms seen in West syndrome).

stem: A 3-year-old girl is evaluated for frequent staring spells and atypical absence seizures. On examination, she has mild microcephaly and a mild ataxic gait. Her EEG reveals 2.5 to 4 Hz generalized spike-and-wave discharges. A lumbar puncture is performed. Which of the following cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) findings would be most diagnostic of her underlying condition?
choices: Elevated protein with normal cell count
Low glucose with normal serum glucose
explanation: Glut1 deficiency syndrome is caused by a defect in glucose transport across the blood-brain barrier. It commonly presents with early-onset absence seizures (before the typical childhood absence age of 4 years), microcephaly, and a movement disorder. The hallmark diagnostic finding is a low CSF glucose level (hypoglycorrhachia) in the setting of a normal serum glucose level.

stem: A 10-year-old boy complains of seeing double. He specifically notes the double vision is much worse when he looks downward, such as when he is walking down a flight of stairs. To compensate for his visual difficulty, he chronically tilts his head to his left side. Which of the following nerves is injured?
explanation: The trochlear nerve (CN IV) innervates the superior oblique muscle, which depresses and intorts the eye. A palsy results in vertical diplopia that is exacerbated on downward gaze (like taking the stairs). Patients intuitively compensate for the extorsion and elevation of the affected eye by tilting their head away from the side of the lesion; therefore, a right CN IV palsy causes a compensatory leftward head tilt.

stem: A 4-month-old infant presents with profound hypotonia, a weak cry, and a "frog-leg" posture. He has never been able to roll over or achieve head control. Examination reveals diffuse areflexia and fasciculations of the tongue. Genetic testing confirms a mutation in the SMN1 gene. What is the pattern of inheritance for this disorder?
explanation: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) Type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffman disease) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the SMN1 gene on chromosome 5. It presents in early infancy with severe lower motor neuron signs (hypotonia, areflexia, tongue fasciculations) and failure to achieve early motor milestones.

stem: A 3-year-old child presents with progressive ataxia, frequent falls, and new-onset seizures. On examination, the child has a pronounced vertical supranuclear gaze palsy and significant hepatosplenomegaly. Which of the following cellular mechanisms is primarily defective in this patient's disease?
choices: Peroxisomal oxidation of very-long-chain fatty acids
explanation: Niemann-Pick Type C is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by an intracellular cholesterol trafficking defect (mutations in NPC1 or NPC2). It frequently presents in childhood with ataxia, hepatosplenomegaly, and a classic vertical gaze apraxia. Diagnosis can be supported by the Filipin staining test, which detects accumulated unesterified cholesterol in lysosomes.

stem: A 6-year-old boy develops bilateral optic neuritis followed a week later by severe paraparesis and sensory loss. MRI of the spine shows a contiguous T2-hyperintense lesion extending through 5 segments of the thoracic cord. Serum testing is negative for Aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies but positive for MOG (Myelin Oligodendrocyte Glycoprotein) antibodies. Which of the following statements regarding his condition is most accurate?
choices: He has a high likelihood of co-existing systemic autoimmune diseases like lupus.
His disease will most likely follow a relentless primary progressive course.
He is likely to have a monophasic course, and he may fully recover.
He is highly likely to develop multiple sclerosis within 5 years.
explanation: Anti-MOG syndrome frequently presents with bilateral optic neuritis, myelitis, or ADEM-like phenotypes in children. Unlike AQP4-positive Neuromyelitis Optica (NMO), anti-MOG disease has a better overall prognosis; it often follows a monophasic course (up to 80% of patients in some cohorts), patients frequently make a full recovery, and it is rarely associated with other systemic autoimmune diseases.

stem: A 14-year-old girl from Finland presents with worsening stimulus-sensitive myoclonus, generalized tonic-clonic seizures, and progressive ataxia. Her MRI brain is normal, and an EEG shows generalized epileptiform discharges. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the CSTB gene encoding cystatin B. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Unverricht-Lundborg disease (Baltic myoclonic epilepsy) is a progressive myoclonic epilepsy that presents between 6 and 15 years of age with stimulus-sensitive myoclonus, seizures, ataxia, and cognitive decline. It is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a mutation in the CSTB gene on chromosome 21, which encodes the protein cystatin B.

stem: A 3-year-old boy is evaluated for severe developmental delay and an almost complete absence of speech. He has an unsteady, jerky gait, frequent hand-flapping movements, and a characteristically happy, frequently laughing demeanor. He also suffers from epilepsy. This syndrome is most commonly caused by which of the following genetic mechanisms?
choices: Trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene
explanation: Angelman syndrome ("happy puppet" syndrome) features severe developmental delay, speech impairment, ataxia, hand-flapping, a happy demeanor, and epilepsy. It is most commonly caused by a microdeletion or inactivation of critical genes on the MATERNALLY inherited chromosome 15q11-q13 (as the paternal genes in this region are normally imprinted/silenced).

stem: A 6-month-old infant of Ashkenazi Jewish descent presents with striking macrocephaly, lack of head control, and profound hypotonia. MRI reveals diffuse, symmetric white matter T2 hyperintensities. Urine organic acid analysis shows markedly elevated levels of N-acetylaspartic acid (NAA). Which enzyme is deficient in this patient?
explanation: Canavan disease is an autosomal recessive leukodystrophy common in the Ashkenazi Jewish population. It is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme aspartoacylase, which leads to the toxic accumulation of N-acetylaspartic acid (NAA) in the brain. The clinical triad features macrocephaly, severe hypotonia, and developmental delay.

stem: A 16-year-old girl with Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy is treated with a first-line broad-spectrum antiepileptic drug. She has been completely seizure-free for 2 years but complains of recent cosmetic and endocrine side effects. Which of the following adverse effects is classically associated with the most likely medication she is taking?
explanation: Valproic acid is highly effective for Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy but carries a heavy side-effect burden in adolescent females. Classically associated adverse effects include weight gain, hair thinning, acne, menstrual irregularities, and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Gingival hyperplasia is associated with phenytoin, while acute angle-closure glaucoma and renal stones are associated with topiramate.

stem: A 12-year-old boy undergoes resection of a benign tumor located deep in his right parietal lobe. Post-operatively, formal visual field testing is performed. Which of the following visual field defects is most likely to be found?
explanation: The superior optic radiations travel through the parietal lobe and carry visual information from the contralateral inferior visual field. A lesion in the right parietal lobe will disrupt these superior fibers, resulting in a left inferior homonymous quadrantanopia (often referred to as a "pie on the floor" defect).

stem: A 2-month-old male infant presents with fluctuating ptosis, ophthalmoparesis, poor feeding, and episodic limb weakness since birth. His mother does not have myasthenia gravis, and his serum is negative for AChR and MuSK antibodies. An EMG with repetitive nerve stimulation reveals a significant decrement. What is the most common genetic etiology for this infant's condition?
explanation: Congenital myasthenic syndromes are a heterogeneous group of genetic disorders of the neuromuscular junction that often present at birth or in early infancy with ptosis, ophthalmoparesis, and hypotonia. The most common form is an autosomal recessive deficiency of the acetylcholine receptor. Acetylcholinesterase deficiency is another variant but is less common.

stem: A newborn infant is noted to have severe generalized hypotonia, facial weakness, a high-arched palate, and respiratory distress requiring immediate intubation. A muscle biopsy is performed, and modified Gomori trichrome staining reveals small, rod-like structures clustered beneath the sarcolemma of the muscle fibers. Which specific muscle fiber type is predominantly affected and atrophic in this condition?
explanation: Nemaline myopathy is a congenital myopathy that can present in the neonatal period with severe hypotonia, dysmorphic facial features, and respiratory distress. Muscle biopsy is diagnostic, showing nemaline rods beneath the sarcolemma. In this condition, Type 1 (slow-oxidative) muscle fibers are predominantly affected and hypotrophic (smaller than normal).

stem: An 8-year-old girl experiences recurrent episodes characterized by seeing multi-colored circles and flashes of light in her left visual field, followed by head version to the left and a hemiclonic seizure. These episodes typically occur during the day and last about 2 to 3 minutes. Her interictal EEG shows high-voltage occipital spikes that disappear with eye opening. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Late-onset childhood occipital epilepsy (Gastaut type) begins around age 8 and features brief, frequent diurnal seizures consisting of elementary visual hallucinations (colored circles/flashes) that may progress to focal motor or hemiclonic seizures. Panayiotopoulos syndrome also has occipital spikes but typically presents in younger children (ages 3-6) predominantly with autonomic symptoms (vomiting, pallor) during sleep.

stem: A 14-year-old girl from Connecticut presents with acute bilateral facial nerve palsies, a severe headache, and neck stiffness. A lumbar puncture reveals a lymphocytic pleocytosis. She recalls having a large, expanding, ring-like red rash on her thigh a month ago after a camping trip. What is the standard treatment for her neurologic condition?
explanation: The patient has Stage 2 (disseminated) Lyme disease caused by Borrelia burgdorferi, presenting with neurologic manifestations (Lyme neuroborreliosis). Classic signs include bilateral cranial nerve VII palsies and lymphocytic meningitis, preceded by the erythema migrans (bull's eye) rash. The standard treatment for Lyme meningitis and cranial neuritis is intravenous ceftriaxone. Here is the sixth batch of 15 high-yield multiple-choice questions for your child neurology board review bank. This set integrates child neurology, epilepsy, neuroanatomy, pharmacology, and adult/pediatric crossover topics, with all explanations directly cited from the provided materials. Child Neurology Board Review Bank: Batch 6 (Questions 71–85)

stem: A 5-day-old full-term infant develops focal clonic seizures that migrate to different parts of the body. The infant is otherwise feeding well and has a normal interictal neurologic examination. EEG is normal between events. The family history is notable for a parent who had similar seizures as a newborn that spontaneously resolved. A mutation in which of the following genes is the most likely cause of this presentation?
explanation: Benign familial neonatal seizures (also known as "fifth day fits") classically present with partial clonic seizures that may migrate to other parts of the body. The condition has an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern and is most commonly caused by mutations in the KCNQ2 or KCNQ3 genes. No treatment is typically necessary as the seizures almost always resolve by 4 to 6 months of life.

stem: A 15-year-old girl presents for evaluation of a first-time generalized tonic-clonic seizure that occurred shortly after she woke up in the morning. Upon further questioning, she admits to frequently dropping her hairbrush in the mornings due to sudden, brief jerks of her arms. Her EEG demonstrates 4 to 6 Hz polyspike-and-wave discharges. Which of the following antiepileptic medications can potentially worsen her condition?
explanation: The patient's clinical presentation (morning myoclonic jerks and a generalized tonic-clonic seizure) and EEG findings (4-6 Hz polyspike-and-wave discharges) are diagnostic of Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy. Medications that are known to worsen myoclonic epilepsies include carbamazepine, gabapentin, pregabalin, lamotrigine, and vigabatrin.

stem: A 14-month-old boy has a brief, generalized tonic-clonic seizure during a febrile illness with a peak temperature of 101.5°F (38.6°C). Which of the following factors specifically increases his risk for having recurrent febrile seizures in the future?
choices: Having complex features during the seizure
Age younger than 18 months at the time of the first febrile seizure
A temperature of 104.0°F (40.0°C) during the current illness
explanation: Risk factors for the recurrence of febrile seizures include being younger than 18 months of age at the time of the first febrile seizure, a lower peak temperature during the illness, a shorter duration of fever prior to the seizure, and a family history of febrile seizures.

stem: A 6-year-old boy with previously normal development presents with progressive loss of language. He no longer responds to his name and appears deaf, though a formal audiogram is normal. An overnight EEG shows anterior epileptiform discharges during sleep. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?
explanation: Landau-Kleffner syndrome, also known as acquired epileptic aphasia, presents with acquired verbal auditory agnosia after a period of normal development. The EEG typically shows anterior discharges during sleep. These patients typically have a good response to corticosteroids.

stem: Neurology (Cortical Malformations) A 10-year-old girl with medically refractory epilepsy and mild intellectual disability undergoes a brain MRI. The MRI demonstrates a thick band of gray matter located deep to the cortex, separated from the true cortex by a layer of white matter. This condition is most commonly associated with a mutation in a gene located on which chromosome?
explanation: Subcortical band heterotopia, or "double cortex," is characterized by a relatively normal cortex with 6 layers, an underlying band of white matter, and then another deep band of gray matter. This condition is seen in patients with DCX gene mutations located on the X chromosome.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is evaluated for severe obesity and hyperphagia. As an infant, he had profound hypotonia requiring gastrostomy tube feeds. On examination, he has a prominent jaw with a thin upper lip and impaired speech development. Genetic testing reveals a microdeletion of chromosome 15q11-q13. This genetic defect is an example of which of the following?
explanation: Prader-Willi syndrome is characterized by profound infantile hypotonia, later hyperphagia, short stature, and distinctive facial features like a prominent jaw and thin upper lip. It is caused by a microdeletion of chromosome 15q11-q13 that exhibits paternal inheritance, meaning the functional genes are only expressed from the paternal chromosome, and the patient only has maternal genes active in this region.

stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with developmental delay, hypotonia, and seizures. Physical examination is notable for prominent alopecia and a skin rash. Laboratory workup reveals ketolactic acidosis and organic aciduria. A defect in which of the following is the most likely cause of this presentation?
explanation: Biotinidase deficiency presents with seizures, ataxia, hypotonia, developmental delay, hearing/vision loss, and cutaneous abnormalities such as alopecia. Laboratory testing typically reveals ketoacidosis, hyperammonemia, and organic aciduria.

stem: A newborn female presents with a giant, abnormally pigmented congenital melanocytic nevus covering her lower back and buttocks. Over the next few months, she develops increasing head circumference and seizures. What is the most common neurologic complication associated with her underlying diagnosis?
explanation: Neurocutaneous melanosis involves various types of abnormally pigmented cutaneous findings (such as giant hair nevi) and leptomeningeal melanosis. In these patients, hydrocephalus is a common neurological complication.

stem: A 16-year-old girl with migraine headaches and focal epilepsy is started on a new daily prophylactic medication. A few months later, she presents to the emergency department with severe flank pain and hematuria, and is diagnosed with renal stones. She also reports recent paresthesias in her fingertips and weight loss. Which medication was she likely prescribed?
explanation: Topiramate is a broad-spectrum antiepileptic drug that acts as a weak carbonic anhydrase inhibitor. Common side effects include paresthesias, decreased appetite, weight loss, and cognitive complaints, while more serious side effects include the promotion of renal stone formation and acute angle glaucoma.

stem: A 15-year-old boy uses sumatriptan for acute migraine relief. Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism of action of this medication class?
explanation: Triptans (such as sumatriptan) are acute treatments for migraine. They work via 5-HT1B receptors to cause constriction of pain-producing, extracerebral blood vessels in the meninges, and via 5-HT1D receptors to inhibit trigeminal peptide release.

stem: A 12-year-old boy presents with headaches and horizontal diplopia. On examination, he is unable to abduct his right eye. The affected cranial nerve is particularly vulnerable to stretching injury from elevated intracranial pressure because of its course over which of the following anatomical structures?
explanation: The abducens nerve (CN VI) controls the lateral rectus muscle and is responsible for eye abduction. It is highly prone to stretching injury, especially as it passes over the petrous ridge, making it the most likely cranial nerve to be involved with elevated intracranial pressure.

stem: A 17-year-old boy with sickle cell disease develops sudden onset of right-sided weakness involving his face and arm much more severely than his leg. He is able to understand commands perfectly but can only produce halting, non-fluent speech. The occluded vessel is most likely a branch of which artery?
explanation: Occlusion of the superior division of the left middle cerebral artery produces hemiparesis (affecting the face and arm more than the leg), gaze preference, and Broca's aphasia (where the patient is able to understand and follow commands but is unable to speak fluently).

stem: A 9-year-old girl develops acute encephalopathy, multifocal weakness, and ataxia two weeks after a viral illness. MRI of the brain and spine shows large, confluent T2-hyperintense lesions in the cerebral white matter and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis. CSF shows a lymphocytic pleocytosis but is negative for oligoclonal bands. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) is a monophasic immunologic reaction to a viral illness that presents with encephalopathy and confluent white matter changes. It can involve the spinal cord with a transverse myelitis appearance, typically affecting more than 3 segments of the cord. Oligoclonal bands are rarely present in the CSF of these patients.

stem: A 4-year-old unvaccinated boy develops rapid-onset flaccid paralysis of his left leg during the month of August. He had a brief febrile illness with GI symptoms 5 days prior. CSF analysis shows a neutrophilic pleocytosis with high protein and normal glucose. MRI of the spine shows T2 hyperintensity in the anterior horns of the spinal cord. Infection with which of the following is most likely?
explanation: West Nile virus is an arbovirus transmitted by mosquitoes that is most common during the summer months. It can cause encephalitis with or without direct invasion of the anterior horn cells, leading to flaccid weakness. The CSF typically shows a neutrophilic pleocytosis with high protein and normal glucose.

stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from the floor. He uses his hands to push himself up his legs (Gowers sign). A muscle biopsy shows a complete absence of dystrophin. Which of the following cardiac manifestations is he at highest risk of developing as he ages?
explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene. In addition to proximal limb and pelvic girdle weakness, cardiac involvement is significant, and patients can develop arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy, and heart failure.

stem: A 7-month-old infant presents with a prolonged, febrile hemiclonic seizure. Over the next year, the child develops multiple seizure types, including myoclonic, atypical absence, and generalized tonic-clonic seizures, along with noticeable developmental delay. Genetic testing is most likely to reveal a mutation in which of the following genes?
explanation: This clinical presentation is classic for Severe Myoclonic Epilepsy of Infancy, also known as Dravet syndrome. Patients typically present with a febrile seizure in the first year of life, then go on to develop other seizure types such as myoclonic, atypical absence, and tonic-clonic seizures. The most frequent genetic cause is a mutation in the SCN1A gene, which represents the severe end of the spectrum of generalized epilepsy with febrile seizures plus.

stem: Neurology (Embryology / Malformations) A neonate is born with midline facial defects including cyclopia and a proboscis. Neuroimaging reveals a single midline cerebral ventricle with fused thalami and an absence of the interhemispheric fissure. This malformation is primarily due to the failure of which embryonic structure to divide?
explanation: Holoprosencephaly is caused by the failure of the prosencephalon to divide into the telencephalon and diencephalon, along with the failure of the diencephalon to divide into two distinct hemispheres. The alobar type is the most severe form, characterized by fused cerebral hemispheres, a single midline ventricle, and variable dysgenesis of the thalamus. It is strongly associated with midline facial defects such as cyclopia (one eye) and proboscis (one nostril), and frequently results from chromosomal defects like Trisomy 13.

stem: Neurology / Neuro-oncology (Paraneoplastic) A 2-year-old child presents with rapid, chaotic conjugate eye movements, severe ataxia, and myoclonus. An abdominal MRI reveals an underlying neuroblastoma. Which of the following statements is true regarding this paraneoplastic syndrome in children compared to adults?
choices: It is most commonly associated with anti-Hu antibodies.
The neurological prognosis is generally better in children than in adults.
Breast cancer is the most common underlying malignancy in children.
It typically presents with a lymphocytic pleocytosis in the cerebrospinal fluid.
explanation: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome can present as a paraneoplastic syndrome. In children, it is classically associated with neuroblastoma and typically lacks a specific paraneoplastic autoantibody, whereas in adults, it is associated with breast, ovarian, or small cell lung cancer and the anti-Ri antibody. The prognosis for this paraneoplastic syndrome is generally better in children, and the primary treatment for the neurological symptoms in children is ACTH.

stem: A 16-year-old female with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy is treated with a broad-spectrum antiepileptic medication. She is currently seizure-free but presents to the clinic complaining of recent bothersome side effects, including weight gain and new-onset menstrual irregularities. Which of the following is another known side effect classically associated with her most likely medication?
explanation: Valproic acid is a broad-spectrum antiepileptic medication frequently used for generalized epilepsies like juvenile myoclonic epilepsy. However, it carries a significant side-effect burden in adolescent females, including weight gain, hair thinning, acne, menstrual irregularities, and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Renal stones and acute angle-closure glaucoma are serious side effects associated with topiramate.

stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with the acute onset of right-sided hemiplegia and a left-sided oculomotor nerve palsy with a dilated, non-reactive pupil. This specific vascular syndrome is most commonly caused by an occlusion of paramedian branches supplying which of the following structures?
explanation: This patient has Weber syndrome, which localizes to the midbrain. It is caused by the occlusion of paramedian branches of the basilar artery supplying the midbrain, leading to an ipsilateral third nerve palsy (with pupillary dilation) and contralateral hemiplegia.

stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with a progressively worsening morning headache, nausea, and ataxia. Brain MRI reveals a large cystic mass with an intensely enhancing mural nodule located in the cerebellum. Pathology demonstrates a biphasic pattern with compact regions containing Rosenthal fibers. What is the World Health Organization (WHO) grade for this tumor?
explanation: Pilocytic astrocytoma is a WHO Grade I tumor and is the most common glioma in children. It most frequently arises in the cerebellum. On MRI, it classically appears as a large cystic portion with an enhancing mural nodule. Pathologically, it features piloid (hair-like) astrocytic processes in a biphasic pattern, often containing characteristic Rosenthal fibers.

stem: A 3-year-old girl is evaluated for frequent staring spells and behavioral arrest. Her EEG reveals generalized spike-and-wave discharges. Because of her unusually early age of onset for absence seizures, she undergoes a lumbar puncture to rule out an underlying metabolic defect. Which condition must be strongly considered and ruled out in this patient?
explanation: Childhood absence epilepsy typically has a peak age of onset of 6 years. If a child presents with the onset of absence seizures before the age of 4 years, GLUT1 deficiency syndrome must be considered. GLUT1 deficiency can present with early-onset absence seizures, microcephaly, and movement disorders, and is diagnosed by finding low CSF glucose with normal serum glucose.

stem: Neurology (Neuromuscular / Genetics) A 4-month-old infant presents with severe generalized hypotonia, a weak cry, and a "frog-leg" posture when placed supine. She is diffusely areflexic and has never been able to achieve head control. This disease is most commonly caused by an autosomal recessive mutation in which of the following genes?
explanation: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) Type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffman disease) presents in early infancy with decreased fetal movements, neonatal hypotonia, weak cry, diffuse areflexia, and a characteristic "frog-leg" posture. It is an autosomal recessive disorder most commonly due to mutations of the SMN1 gene on chromosome 5, which is involved in RNA processing.

stem: A 2-week-old neonate presents with frequent epileptic tonic spasms occurring dozens of times per day. The EEG reveals a burst-suppression pattern that is present continuously during both wakefulness and sleep. Structural brain malformations are identified on MRI. What is the most accurate diagnosis?
explanation: Ohtahara syndrome, or early infantile epileptic encephalopathy, typically begins between day 1 and month 3 of life. It is characterized by frequent epileptic tonic spasms and an EEG showing a burst-suppression pattern that occurs during both wakefulness and sleep. Structural brain malformations are the most common etiology. In contrast, Early Myoclonic Encephalopathy typically features a burst-suppression pattern predominantly during sleep, prominent myoclonic seizures, and is more frequently caused by metabolic disorders.

stem: A 1-month-old female infant is evaluated for new-onset focal seizures. Examination reveals a vesicular and verrucous rash following Blaschko lines on her trunk and extremities. The mother reports a history of multiple spontaneous abortions of male fetuses. This disorder is caused by a mutation in a gene encoding a protein for which of the following cellular pathways?
explanation: Incontinentia Pigmenti is an X-linked dominant condition that is typically lethal in males. It is caused by a mutation of the NEMO gene, which encodes a protein involved in the nuclear factor kappa B pathway. In affected females, skin lesions progress through stages (vesiculobullous, verrucous, hyperpigmented) that follow Blaschko lines, and patients can suffer from seizures, cognitive dysfunction, and retinal issues.

stem: A 12-year-old immigrant boy with an unknown vaccination history presents with progressive cognitive decline, behavioral changes, and prominent myoclonic jerks. An EEG reveals periodic high-amplitude slow-wave complexes occurring at regular intervals. He had a severe febrile rash illness at age 1. What is the characteristic pathological finding in this patient's brain?
choices: Neurons containing nuclear and cytoplasmic viral inclusion bodies
explanation: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) is a rapidly progressive, fatal dementing process caused by a defective measles virus infection. Children infected in the first 2 years of life carry the highest risk, and symptoms typically develop 2 to 12 years after the initial infection. Pathologically, neurons contain characteristic nuclear and cytoplasmic viral inclusion bodies.

stem: Following a difficult vaginal delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia, a large-for-gestational-age neonate is noted to hold his right arm internally rotated and adducted at the shoulder, with the elbow extended. This "waiter's tip" posture is caused by an injury that primarily involves which of the following nerve roots?
explanation: Erb's palsy (an upper trunk lesion) results in the arm being held in a "waiter's tip" position. This commonly occurs when the shoulder is forcefully pulled down while the neck is flexed in the opposite direction during delivery. The upper trunk of the brachial plexus is formed by the junction of the C5 and C6 nerve roots at Erb's point.

stem: An 11-year-old girl is prescribed sumatriptan nasal spray for the acute abortive treatment of her severe migraines. The primary mechanism of action of this medication involves agonism at which of the following receptors?
explanation: Triptans (such as sumatriptan) are 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptor agonists. Agonism at 5-HT1B causes constriction of pain-producing, extracerebral blood vessels in the meninges, while agonism at 5-HT1D inhibits trigeminal peptide release and interferes with trigeminal nucleus caudalis transduction and processing.

stem: A 4-year-old boy wakes up in the middle of the night looking pale and terrified. He complains of nausea, vomits forcefully, and then exhibits eye deviation to the right and unresponsiveness lasting for 10 minutes. His EEG shows high-voltage occipital spikes that disappear with eye opening and are strongly activated by sleep. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Panayiotopoulos syndrome is a childhood epilepsy syndrome with a peak age of 3 to 6 years. It is characterized predominantly by autonomic symptoms (pallor, severe nausea, and prominent vomiting) followed by eye deviation and impairment of consciousness. Seizures most commonly occur during sleep. The classic EEG finding is high-voltage occipital spikes in 1-3 Hz bursts that disappear with eye opening and are activated by sleep.

stem: Neurology (Metabolic / Leukodystrophy) A 3-year-old girl with previously normal development presents with progressive clumsiness, frequent falls, and a loss of language skills. Examination shows spasticity in her legs but absent deep tendon reflexes at the knees and ankles. MRI of the brain shows symmetric T2 hyperintensities in the periventricular white matter that notably spare the subcortical U-fibers. Laboratory testing will most likely show a deficiency of which enzyme?
explanation: Metachromatic leukodystrophy is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a deficiency of arylsulfatase A, which normally breaks down cerebroside sulfate. The accumulation of sulfatide leads to widespread demyelination of both the central and peripheral nervous systems. The late infantile form presents between 2 and 4 years of age with progressive psychomotor slowing, ataxia, and seizures. MRI classically shows T2 hyperintense signal changes in the periventricular and subcortical white matter that spare the U-fibers. Here is the next batch of 15 questions. As requested, these feature slightly shorter clinical stems and more concise explanations, making them excellent for rapid-fire review. They remain heavily focused on child neurology while integrating pharmacology, neuroanatomy, and crossover topics. Child Neurology Board Review Bank: Batch 7 (Questions 101–115)

stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with developmental delay, fair skin, and a musty odor to his urine. If left untreated, this condition leads to progressive intellectual disability. Which of the following enzymes is most likely deficient?
explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by a deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase, preventing the normal conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine. This leads to the accumulation of phenylacetic acid, which is responsible for the classic musty odor of the sweat and urine.

stem: A 7-year-old boy wakes up with right-sided facial twitching, drooling, and an inability to speak. The episode lasts for 2 minutes. His EEG shows bilateral independent centrotemporal spikes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: Benign childhood epilepsy with centrotemporal spikes
explanation: Benign childhood epilepsy with centrotemporal spikes (BECTS), or Rolandic epilepsy, typically presents between ages 2 and 13. Seizures frequently occur during sleep or upon waking, often featuring facial twitching, drooling, and speech arrest.

stem: A 6-year-old girl is evaluated in the clinic. She has 8 café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, and Lisch nodules on her irises. She is at the highest risk for developing which of the following central nervous system tumors?
explanation: Neurofibromatosis type 1 is diagnosed clinically by features like café-au-lait macules, Lisch nodules, and axillary/inguinal freckling. These patients have a significantly increased risk of developing optic pathway gliomas.
